records.ety: [OE. blome, fr. Icel. blm, blmi; akin to Sw. blom, Goth. bl<?/ma, OS. bl<?/mo, D. bloem, OHG. bluomo, bluoma, G. blume; fr. the same root as AS. bl<?/wan to blow, blossom. See Blow to bloom, and cf. Blossom.]

defs: A blossom; the flower of a plant; an expanded bud; flowers, collectively.
quotes: The rich blooms of the tropics.

defs: The opening of flowers in general; the state of blossoming or of having the flowers open; as, the cherry trees are in bloom.
A state or time of beauty, freshness, and vigor; an opening to higher perfection, analogous to that of buds into blossoms; as, the bloom of youth.
quotes: Every successive mother has transmitted a fainter bloom, a more delicate and briefer beauty.

defs: The delicate, powdery coating upon certain growing or newly-gathered fruits or leaves, as on grapes, plums, etc. Hence: Anything giving an appearance of attractive freshness; a flush; a glow.
quotes: A new, fresh, brilliant world, with all the bloom upon it.

defs: The clouded appearance which varnish sometimes takes upon the surface of a picture.
A yellowish deposit or powdery coating which appears on well-tanned leather.
A popular term for a bright-hued variety of some minerals; as, the rose-red cobalt bloom.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Bloomed (<?/); p. pr. & vb. n. Blooming.]

defs: To produce or yield blossoms; to blossom; to flower or be in flower.
quotes: A flower which once In Paradise, fast by the tree of life, Began to bloom.

defs: To be in a state of healthful, growing youth and vigor; to show beauty and freshness, as of flowers; to give promise, as by or with flowers.
quotes: A better country blooms to view,

defs: To cause to blossom; to make flourish.
To bestow a bloom upon; to make blooming or radiant.
quotes: While barred clouds bloom the soft-dying day.

records.ety: [AS. bl<?/ma a mass or lump, īsenes bl<?/ma a lump or wedge of iron.]

defs: A mass of wrought iron from the Catalan forge or from the puddling furnace, deprived of its dross, and shaped usually in the form of an oblong block by shingling.
A large bar of steel formed directly from an ingot by hammering or rolling, being a preliminary shape for further working.
